1: 'Do not envy evil people' — the temptation of the young to admire the apparent freedom and success of those who disregard wisdom's constraints. In the ANE patronage world, the wicked could achieve visible prosperity through corruption; the wisdom tradition had to address the psychological pull of that success directly.
2: 'Their heart plans violence' — the interior reality behind the evil person's apparent success. The wise person sees through the surface prosperity to the destructive orientation beneath. Heart (lev) and lips (sefataim) as the interior and exterior of the evil person — both corrupted.
3: 'By wisdom a house is built, and by understanding it is established' — the building metaphor for wisdom's constructive social role. A 'house' in the ANE was not merely a physical structure but a dynastic unit: the house of David, the house of such-and-such. Wisdom-building is the building of lasting social structures and family lines.
4: 'Through knowledge the rooms are filled with precious and pleasant treasures' — the interior filling that follows the structural establishment. The progression (build, establish, fill) mirrors the creation narrative of Gen 1 (form, then fill). Wisdom is cosmogonic in its operation: it brings order and abundance.
5: 'A wise man is strong' (chacham be'oz) — wisdom as a form of power. In ANE court culture, the sage's power was precisely intellectual: strategic counsel, knowledge of precedent, ability to read situations. The emphasis on wisdom-as-strength counters the assumption that physical or military force was the only meaningful power.
6: 'By wise guidance you can wage war' (betachbulot taasseh lekha milchamah) — tachbulot (wise guidance, steering) is a nautical metaphor: the pilot's skill in maneuvering a ship. Applied to warfare, it describes strategic rather than merely tactical intelligence. 'Victory depends on having many advisers' — ANE kings maintained councils of wise men for precisely this reason.
7: 'Wisdom is beyond a fool's reach; he has nothing to say at the city gate' — the gate as the venue for public deliberation. The fool's inability to contribute at the gate (the civic and legal assembly) marks his social disqualification. Wisdom was not merely private virtue but the qualification for public participation in ANE city life.
8: 'Whoever schemes to do evil will be known as a troublemaker' (ba'al mezimmot) — the one who devises evil plans. The social exposure of the schemer — 'will be known' — reflects the tight-knit community in which reputation was impossible to fully conceal over time. Evil schemes eventually identify their authors.
9: 'The intention behind foolish schemes is sin' — the moral evaluation reaches inside the planning stage. ANE legal codes generally focused on action; Proverbs' wisdom tradition extends the moral assessment backward to intention, a development that anticipates the NT's heart-ethics.
10: 'If you falter in times of trouble, how small is your strength' (im hitrafita beyom tsarah tsar kochekha) — the test of character reveals its depth. 'Trouble' (tsarah) is the Proverbs and Psalms word for the affliction that tests — the genuine crisis in which wisdom's reservoir is either drawn on or found empty. Performance under pressure reveals genuine versus nominal formation.
11: 'Rescue those being taken away to death; hold back those stumbling toward slaughter' — an active obligation to intervene on behalf of those facing unjust execution. In ancient judicial systems, capital sentences could be carried out swiftly and sometimes unjustly; the bystander obligation to intervene was a wisdom-tradition mandate, not merely a personal option.
12: 'Does not he who weighs the heart perceive it?' — the divine heart-reader as the answer to the 'I did not know' defense. In ANE contexts, ignorance was often a legal defense. Proverbs responds: the one who knows hearts (tochen libot, the 'weigher of hearts') — a divine attribute — renders that defense inadequate before God even if it succeeds before human courts.
13: 'My son, eat honey because it is good' — honey as an analogy for wisdom. Honey was the most prized sweet in ancient Israel before cane sugar; finding a honeycomb was a significant discovery (1 Samuel 14:25-29; Psalms 19:10). Its natural sweetness made it the obvious sensory analogy for the pleasure of wisdom's discovery.
14: 'So is wisdom to your soul; when you find it, there is a future' (acharit vetiqvatekha lo tikaret) — the same future-hope formula as Proverbs 23:18. The pairing of honey-pleasure with future-hope addresses both the immediate and long-term dimensions of wisdom: it is pleasant now and secures the future. The two-dimensional appeal was the standard ANE wisdom incentive structure.
15: 'Do not lie in wait against the home of the righteous' — warning to the wicked against targeting the righteous person's household. The household (naveh, abode, pasture) as the unit of social security; attacking it is an attack on the righteous person's entire social existence. The specific prohibition addresses predatory wealth-transfer through legal manipulation or violence.
16: 'A righteous person falls seven times and gets up again, but the wicked are brought down by calamity' — one of Proverbs' most memorable characterizations of moral resilience. 'Seven times' (sheva yipol) is the number of completeness — every conceivable fall. The righteous person's defining characteristic is not imperviousness to setback but recovery capacity. The wicked's single calamity finishes them; the righteous survive multiple ones.
17: 'Do not gloat when your enemy falls' — the prohibition of schadenfreude. In the ANE honor-shame system, the public downfall of an enemy was a social victory to be celebrated. Proverbs counters this: the wise person's response to an enemy's defeat is moral restraint, not public celebration. The reason given is theological: God's response to human gloating affects his justice-calculation.
18: 'Lest the LORD see it and be displeased, and he turns his anger away from your enemy' — the theological mechanism: God's displeasure at gloating may lead him to stop punishing the enemy. The proverb teaches that vindictive satisfaction at an enemy's suffering can actually undermine the justice one wanted — a sophisticated counter to the revenge impulse.
19: 'Do not fret because of evildoers or be envious of the wicked' — reprising the counsel of Proverbs 24:1, now as a double prohibition: do not agitate or envy. The ANE wisdom tradition repeatedly addresses the psychological distress caused by observed injustice — the temptation either to worry about systemic unfairness or to envy those who exploit it.
20: 'The evil person has no future reward; the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out' — the lamp (ner) as the image of life and household vitality (cf. Proverbs 13:9, Proverbs 20:20). In ancient homes, the household lamp was kept burning through the night as a sign of life; its extinction was a potent symbol of death and dynastic extinction.
21: 'Fear the LORD and the king, and do not associate with those who seek to undermine either' — the double loyalty of the wise person: to divine and human authority. ANE court wisdom consistently taught loyalty to the ruling power as a practical and moral obligation. The parallel structure places fear of the LORD and fear of the king in the same category — not equating them but recognizing both as legitimate authority structures.
22: 'Their disaster will come suddenly, and who knows what destruction may come from both?' — the unpredictability of both divine and royal punishment for those who undermine authority. The wise person does not need to calculate timing; the certainty of eventual disaster is sufficient motivation for loyalty.
23: Proverbs 24:23-34 forms a small appendix: 'these also are sayings of the wise' (gam elleh lachachamim) — indicating a secondary collection. The opening concern is judicial impartiality, the foundational legal principle of the Mosaic law (Deuteronomy 16:19; Leviticus 19:15). Partiality in judgment corrupts the entire system on which community order depends.
24: 'Whoever tells the guilty, You are innocent, will be cursed by peoples and condemned by nations' — the international scope of the condemnation is striking: nations curse the corrupt judge. In the ANE context where Israel interacted with surrounding peoples, legal reputation was not merely a domestic matter. Judicial corruption undermined the entire society's claim to moral order.
25: 'Those who rebuke the wicked will find delight' — the positive reward for judicial courage. Rebuking the wicked in a court context was socially risky; the wise person who did so anyway received both personal satisfaction (naim, delight) and divine blessing (birkat tov, a good blessing).
26: 'Whoever gives an honest answer is like a kiss on the lips' — the intimacy and pleasure of truthful speech. The kiss (nashaq) in the ANE context was both a greeting between intimates and a gesture of covenant-making. An honest answer in a difficult situation is as welcome and as relationship-building as a kiss.
27: 'Prepare your outdoor work and get your fields ready; after that, build your house' — the sequence of priorities: productive capacity before domestic comfort. In an agricultural economy, the field is the income-generator; the house is consumption. Establishing the productive base before building the household reflects the wisdom that sustainable prosperity requires productive infrastructure.
28: 'Do not testify against your neighbor without cause' — the legal protection against false witness. The Mosaic law's ninth commandment (Exodus 20:16) is here given its practical wisdom form. In ancient communities without formal investigation procedures, false witness was easy and devastating; the prohibition had to be reiterated in multiple forms.
29: 'Do not say, I will do to him what he did to me; I will repay him' — the retaliatory impulse addressed directly. The wisdom tradition counters both private vengeance (leave it to God and the courts) and the anticipatory planning of retribution ('do not say'). The planning stage of revenge is the point at which wisdom must intervene.
30: The sluggard's field (Proverbs 24:30-34) is a wisdom poem using observed experience as the basis for instruction. 'I passed by the field of a lazy man' — the first-person observation is the wisdom teacher's method: he walks through the world with eyes open and draws lessons from what he sees. The field is a specific, visible object lesson.
31: 'All overgrown with thorns; the ground covered with nettles; the stone wall in ruins' — the three-fold description of agricultural abandonment. Each element represents a specific failure of maintenance: cleared ground (to thorns), tilled ground (to nettles), built structures (to ruin). The comprehensive neglect is the visible result of the invisible character flaw.
32: 'I saw it and took it to heart; I looked and received instruction' (vaetbonena lakachti musar) — the wisdom method: attentive observation becomes moral formation. Musar (instruction, discipline) is received not from a teacher but from a ruined field. Creation itself is a teacher for the one with eyes to see.
33: 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest' — the incremental nature of sloth. Each step is small and individually justifiable; the cumulative effect is catastrophic. The repetition of 'a little' (meat) three times mimics the self-deceptive reasoning of the sluggard who never makes a decisive choice for inaction but drifts there through small compromises.
34: 'Then poverty will come on you like a wanderer, and your need like an armed man' — the suddenness and violence of poverty's arrival. The wanderer (mehalekh) appears without warning; the armed man (ish magen) cannot be resisted. The proverb's conclusion matches Proverbs 6:9-11; the same observation closes both the earlier and later collections — its repetition signals its importance as a foundational wisdom axiom.
